epigraph.text:
He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has 
also set eternity in the human heart; yet no one can fathom what God has 
done from beginning to end.
epigraph.quote: Pure mathematics is, in its way, the poetry of logical ideas.

content.text: {Pure mathematics is, in its way, the poetry of logical ideas.}{Albert Einstein}

In this chapter, we explored inequalities—mathematical statements that 
describe ranges, constraints, and boundaries rather than exact values. 
Just as God has set boundaries throughout creation to enable life to 
flourish, inequalities allow us to model the constraints that govern 
the natural and human-designed world.

items:
Graphing on a number line: open circles for $<$ and $>$; 
 closed circles for $\leq$ and $\geq$

Translating verbal phrases to inequalities
 itemize

**Section 4.2: Solving One-Step and Two-Step Inequalities**
 itemize
Addition and subtraction do not change inequality direction
Multiplication/division by positives do not change direction

**Critical rule:** Multiplying or dividing by a negative 
 **reverses** the inequality symbol

Always check solutions by substituting values
 itemize
Use the distributive property when needed
Collect variables on one side when they appear on both sides
Clear fractions by multiplying by the LCD

Special cases: all real numbers or no solution
 itemize

Special cases: no solution (empty intersection) or all reals 
 (complete union)
 itemize
Isolate the absolute value first when needed

Applications in tolerance and error analysis
 itemize

content.text: Graph each inequality on a number line.
Write each inequality in set-builder notation and interval notation.
Translate each statement into an inequality.

items: The speed must be at most 55 mph.
The age must be at least 21.

content.text: Section 4.2: Solving One-Step and Two-Step Inequalities
Solve each inequality. Graph the solution and write it in interval notation.

Determine whether each has all real numbers, no solution, or a 
restricted solution set.
Solve each inequality and write the solution in interval notation.
Solve each inequality and write the solution in interval notation.

A student needs at least 400 points to earn an A in a class. After 
three assignments worth 100 points each, the student has earned 82, 
91, and 78 points. What is the minimum score needed on the fourth 
assignment to earn an A?

A phone plan charges \$35 per month plus \$0.05 per text message. 
If you want to keep your bill under \$50, what is the maximum number 
of text messages you can send?

The ideal length for a manufactured component is 12.5 cm with a 
tolerance of $\pm 0.3$ cm. Write and solve an absolute value inequality 
for acceptable lengths.

A healthy cholesterol level is between 125 and 200 mg/dL. Write this 
as a compound inequality.

An amusement park ride requires riders to be at least 48 inches but 
no more than 78 inches tall. Write this as a compound inequality.

A weather alert is issued when the temperature is below 0°F or above 
100°F. Write this as a compound inequality describing normal (non-alert) 
temperatures.

content.title: Preparing for the Chapter Test
content.content:
1. Review all "Try It" problems from each section.

2. Rework any homework problems you found challenging.

3. Pay special attention to:
 itemize

4. Flipping the inequality when multiplying/dividing by negatives

5. Choosing correct circles (open vs. closed) when graphing

6. Setting up absolute value inequalities correctly

7. Distinguishing "and" from "or" in compound inequalities
 itemize

8. Practice writing solutions in all three forms: inequality, 
 interval notation, and graph.

9. For word problems, identify the inequality type first, then 
 set up and solve.

content.text:
*Complete without notes. Check your answers, then review any 
concepts you missed.*

items: Graph $x \geq -2$ on a number line and write in interval notation.
Solve $4x - 7 > 13$. Graph and express in interval notation.

A recipe requires the oven temperature to be between 325°F and 
 375°F. Write this as a compound inequality.

A metal rod must be 8.00 cm long with a tolerance of $\pm 0.05$ cm. 
 Write and solve an absolute value inequality for acceptable lengths.

A taxi charges \$2.50 plus \$0.40 per quarter mile. If you have 
 \$15, write and solve an inequality for the maximum distance you 
 can travel.
Solve $3x + 5 \leq 3(x + 2)$. Identify if this is a special case.
Solve $|x + 1| < -3$. Identify if this is a special case.

content.text:
In the next chapter, we will study **functions**—rules that assign 
exactly one output to each input. Inequalities will appear again when we 
discuss the *domain* of a function (which input values are allowed) 
and the *range* (which output values are possible). The concepts 
of constraints and boundaries you learned in this chapter will help you 
understand where functions are defined and how they behave.

Functions are the language of relationships in mathematics. Just as 
inequalities describe *where* things can happen, functions describe 
*what* happens—how inputs transform into outputs according to 
consistent rules. Together, these tools will enable you to model and 
analyze the world with increasing precision and power.

marginNotes.text:
To all perfection I see a limit, but 
your commands are boundless.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
Nothing takes place in the world whose 
meaning is not that of some maximum or minimum.
